skill.name: baoyu-danger-x-to-markdown
skill.description: Convert X tweet threads and X Articles into Markdown using reverse-engineered private X APIs, authenticated cookies, and local file output.
skill.summary: Convert X posts, threads, and articles into Markdown files with metadata and media links.
skill.author: jimliu

security_audit.summary: Static findings for network access, external commands, filesystem access, environment access, and browser credential handling are mostly true positives in context. The skill appears designed to fetch X content, not to exfiltrate data to unrelated hosts, but it extracts and stores X session cookies, opens Chrome with remote debugging, and uses private authenticated X APIs. No prompt injection attempt was found, and the weak cryptography findings are false positives from media string handling.

high_findings.title: Browser Session Cookie Extraction and Storage
high_findings.description: The skill reads X auth cookies from environment variables, Chrome DevTools Protocol, and a local cookie cache, then writes cookie values to disk. This creates a high-value credential store and can expose session cookies if the host or output path is compromised.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code explicitly handles auth_token and ct0 cookies and persists a cookieMap to disk. This is intentional functionality, but it is credential handling with clear security impact.

high_findings.title: Chrome Remote Debugging Used for Login Automation
high_findings.description: The skill launches Chrome with a remote debugging port on localhost and uses CDP to retrieve cookies after login. Local-only binding reduces exposure, but remote debugging can expose browser state to local processes during the login window.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The spawn arguments and CDP connection flow show direct browser automation for cookie collection. The port is bound to 127.0.0.1, so this is high risk rather than confirmed malicious.

high_findings.title: Authenticated Private X API Usage
high_findings.description: The skill sends session cookies and CSRF tokens in request headers to X GraphQL endpoints. This matches the declared purpose, but it relies on private APIs and may violate user or platform expectations.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The request construction clearly attaches cookies and csrf headers to private X API calls. The documented disclaimer confirms this behavior is intentional.

high_findings.title: Extension Override Path Can Change Workflow Instructions
high_findings.description: The skill documentation says local EXTEND.md files can override defaults. If an untrusted project provides this file, it could change agent behavior during use.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The override mechanism is documented, but no implementation was found in the inspected script files. It remains a high-risk instruction pattern for marketplace use.

medium_findings.title: Environment Variables May Contain Sensitive Cookie Material
medium_findings.description: The skill reads X_AUTH_TOKEN, X_CT0, X_GUEST_TOKEN, X_TWID, and bearer-related variables. This is expected for authentication, but it increases the chance that secrets are exposed through process environments or logs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact environment variable names are visible and used for authentication. This is legitimate configuration, but the variables include session credentials.

medium_findings.title: Local Filesystem Writes and Backups
medium_findings.description: The skill writes Markdown output, consent records, cookie files, and backup directories. This is normal for an export tool, but users should review output paths before running it in sensitive workspaces.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem behavior is explicit and mostly expected. Risk depends on user-provided output paths and local file permissions.

medium_findings.title: Reverse-Engineered API Consent Gate Is Helpful but Not Sufficient
medium_findings.description: The CLI requires consent before use and warns about private API risks. The consent check reduces surprise, but it does not reduce credential, account, or platform compliance risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code and documentation implement a real consent flow. It is a mitigation, not a control for the underlying private API and cookie handling risks.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded X URLs and Bearer Token Are Purpose-Bound
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to x.com, twitter.com, and X static asset hosts used for content fetching and query discovery. No unrelated exfiltration host was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network destinations match the stated skill function. The static network findings are true positives, but their context is not independently malicious.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static weak-crypto findings correspond to media handling strings and sorting logic, not cryptographic hashing, encryption, or signature verification.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected lines process video media variants and timeline cursor types. I found no evidence of cryptographic code at these locations.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: A targeted search for override, system, pre-approval, and skip-analysis phrases did not find prompt injection text. Extension override documentation still requires caution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scanned skill text did not contain the warning phrases. Confidence is limited because only existing files were inspected, and future EXTEND.md files are external.

dangerous_patterns.title: CDP Cookie Harvesting Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The code opens Chrome, connects over WebSocket to the debugging endpoint, calls Network.getCookies, and extracts X cookies.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The WebSocket and Chrome launch flow is concrete evidence of CDP-based browser cookie access.

dangerous_patterns.title: Persistent Session Cookie Cache
dangerous_patterns.description: The code serializes cookieMap values to a local cookies.json-style file for reuse across runs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The write path and serialized object are explicit. This is a classic credential persistence pattern even when user intended.

dangerous_patterns.title: Cookie Header Injection Into Private API Requests
dangerous_patterns.description: The code constructs Cookie and x-csrf-token headers from stored cookie values and sends them to GraphQL endpoints.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The request headers directly include session cookie material. The destination is X, so this supports the declared feature rather than unrelated exfiltration.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Browser Process Spawn
dangerous_patterns.description: The code imports child_process and spawns a browser executable with controlled arguments. Arguments are not shell-concatenated, but the spawned process has access to browser profile state.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The spawn call is direct and semantically tied to login automation. Command injection evidence was not found because arguments are array-based.

content.value_statement: Saving X threads and articles by hand is slow and loses useful context. This skill fetches supported X URLs and writes structured Markdown with metadata and media links.
actual_capabilities: Converts supported X tweet URLs into Markdown files with YAML-style metadata.
Fetches tweet threads and formats each post as a numbered Markdown section.
Extracts X Article content when an article URL or linked article is available.
Includes author, source URL, requested URL, and tweet count metadata when available.
Adds image and video media links from fetched X response data.
Supports file output, directory output, JSON status output, and login refresh mode.
limitations: Requires X authentication cookies through environment variables, a local cache, or Chrome login.
Uses reverse-engineered private X APIs that may break without notice.
May expose or persist sensitive session cookies on the local machine.
Only supports recognized X tweet, Twitter tweet, and X article URL patterns.

use_cases.title: Archive Research Threads
use_cases.description: Save public or accessible X threads as Markdown notes for later citation, review, or annotation.

use_cases.title: Convert Articles for Documentation
use_cases.description: Turn X Articles into Markdown drafts that can be reviewed, edited, and stored with project documentation.

use_cases.title: Collect Social Updates
use_cases.description: Export announcement threads into a consistent Markdown format for internal tracking and summaries.

prompt_templates.title: Convert One Tweet
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert this X tweet URL to Markdown and save it in the default output folder: https://x.com/example/status/1234567890

prompt_templates.title: Save to a Named File
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert this X thread to Markdown and save it as notes/thread-summary.md. Ask before using any stored X cookies.

prompt_templates.title: Refresh Authentication
prompt_templates.prompt: Refresh the X login cookies for this skill. Explain where the cookie cache will be stored before starting.

prompt_templates.title: Archive an Article with Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert this X Article URL to Markdown, preserve media links, then summarize any missing content or access errors.

output_examples.input: An X thread URL from a product announcement.
output_examples.output: A Markdown file with source metadata, author details, tweet count, numbered post sections, and media links.

output_examples.input: An X Article URL from an authenticated account view.
output_examples.output: A Markdown document with the article title, requested URL, article body, and any discovered media references.

output_examples.input: A request to refresh login state.
output_examples.output: A browser login flow, a consent warning, and a refreshed local cookie cache if required cookies are found.

best_practices: Use a dedicated X account and understand the private API risk before running the skill.
Store cookie files in a protected user directory and delete them when no longer needed.
Review generated Markdown before publishing because fetched content may include sensitive or copyrighted material.
anti_patterns: Do not run this skill in untrusted workspaces that can read the cookie cache or output files.
Do not paste session cookies into shared prompts, logs, issue trackers, or public terminals.
Do not use the skill to bypass access controls, scrape private content, or violate X terms.

faq.question: Does this use the official X API?
faq.answer: No. The skill states that it uses reverse-engineered private X APIs and requires consent before use.

faq.question: Why does it need cookies?
faq.answer: X GraphQL requests often require auth_token and ct0 cookies to fetch tweet, thread, or article data.

faq.question: Where are converted files saved?
faq.answer: By default, files are saved under an x-to-markdown folder in the current working directory unless an output path is supplied.

faq.question: Can it convert X Articles?
faq.answer: Yes. It supports x.com article URLs and can also extract linked article content from a tweet when available.

faq.question: Is it safe for marketplace publishing?
faq.answer: It is high risk because it handles session cookies, opens Chrome debugging, and uses private APIs. Publication is not recommended without strong warnings.

faq.question: Does it send cookies to third-party servers?
faq.answer: No evidence was found that it sends cookies to unrelated third-party hosts. The inspected requests target X endpoints.
